title: Chapter 28: The Warning Shot
content: The Foundation is different after midnight. The security lights cast everything in amber wash, turning the studios into caves of half-formed garments and dormant dress forms. I like it this way — the quiet, the emptiness, the sense that the building has exhaled and won't inhale again until morning. I've been hand-finishing the seam on my showcase piece, a structured coat that borrows from the silhouette of a 1780s justaucorps but refracts it through something sharper, more modern. The needlework is meditative. Pull, thread, knot. Pull, thread, knot. A rhythm older than most of the buildings in this city.
I don't hear her approach. I smell her first.

Vetiver — dark, earthy, with a green edge that cuts through the dust of fabric and starch. It arrives before she does, announcing her like a herald. I look up from my needle and Maren Voss is standing in my studio doorway.
No preamble. No pleasantries. No tilt of the head or thin smile to soften the intrusion.
"We need to talk."

Her voice is silk over steel — controlled, precise, each word placed with the deliberation of a chess piece being set down. Not loud. Not soft. Calibrated. I've heard that calibration before, in interrogation rooms and court chambers and the quiet offices of men who held my fate in ink-stained fingers. The ones who were most dangerous never raised their voices.

My instinct is to run. It's always run. Four centuries of practice and the impulse still fires like a reflex — the prickle at the nape, the shift of weight toward the balls of my feet, the calculation of exits. But I've also had four centuries of learning to override it. I stay in my chair. I set down my needle. I keep my hands visible and my breathing even.
"About what?"
Maren steps inside. She reaches behind her and closes the door. The click of the latch is small and final.
"About you."

She crosses the studio with the unhurried confidence of someone who has already won. Her heels are silent on the concrete — she changed into flats at some point, or perhaps she arrived in them, planning this. Planning me. She pulls out the chair across from my worktable and sits, and the gesture is so casual, so possessive, that for a disorienting moment it feels like her studio. Like I'm the visitor.
She places her phone on the table between us. Face up. The screen glows.
Two photographs side by side.

On the left: a wartime photograph from 1943. Black and white, slightly overexposed, the kind taken by newspaper correspondents who moved fast and rarely asked permission. A woman at a Paris café, head turned three-quarters toward the camera, one hand lifted to adjust the brim of a wide hat. The jaw. The orbit of the eyes. The goddamn cheekbones.

On the right: a paparazzi shot from the Met Gala, barely three weeks old. Me in the Ashworth dress, caught mid-turn on the steps, the flash bleaching color from my skin but leaving every angle intact.
The bone structure is identical. Not similar. Not reminiscent. Identical. The same skeleton under different lighting, different decades, different everything.

My heart is a fist punching the inside of my ribs. I keep my face perfectly still. I have practiced this stillness — in the torture chambers of Seville, in the examining rooms of Vienna, in every place where showing fear was the same as surrendering to it. My expression is water. My pulse is war.

"I've been doing research," Maren says. She's watching me the way a hawk watches a field mouse — not with malice, not yet, but with the patience of something that knows its prey cannot escape. "Your face appears in records spanning three hundred years. The same skeleton. The same eyes. The same goddamn cheekbones."
"People have lookalikes." My voice comes out level. I'm almost proud of it. "It's not unusual. There are websites devoted to it. Döppelgangers. Gene expression—"
"This isn't resemblance." Maren's fingers tap the screen, zooming in on the 1943 photograph. The cheekbone, the brow, the particular dip of the cupid's bow. "This is replication."

I look at the photograph. I remember that day. I remember the café — Le Départ, near Montparnasse — and I remember the correspondent who took the shot, a Canadian man with kind eyes and terrible aim. I remember the hat, which I'd stolen from a collaborator's closet. I remember that Paris smelled of cigarettes and fear and the particular sweetness of liberated wine.
I do not say any of this.
I say: "Photographs from the 1940s are grainy. The resolution makes comparison unreliable."
Maren tilts her head. A thin smile, barely there. She expected this. She prepared for it.

"I didn't come with one photograph, Orlando." She swipes. Another image. A painting this time — oil on canvas, 1879, the portrait of a Florentine merchant's wife. The cheekbones. The jaw. The particular slant of the eyes that I've seen in mirrors across centuries. Swipe. A daguerreotype from 1852. Swipe. A society photograph from 1967. Swipe. Swipe. Swipe.
Each one a different era. Each one my face.

"The Metropolitan Museum's digital archive," Maren says. "The New York Public Library's picture collection. Getty Images. Ancestry.com genealogical records. It wasn't even hard." She says this with the mild surprise of someone who expected a challenge and found the door unlocked. "You didn't hide. You never hid. You simply... assumed no one would look."

The metallic taste rises in the back of my throat — fear's own chemistry, copper and iron, the body betraying what the face conceals. I swallow it down. I keep my hands flat on the table. My nails press half-moons into my palms.

Maren leans forward. She's not afraid. She's not confused. She's not even curious, not really — curiosity implies uncertainty, and Maren Voss is certain. She is calculating. Predators don't fear their prey; they measure them.

"I don't know what you are." She says this plainly, as if cataloging a specimen. "A genetic anomaly. A fraud. A very elaborate grifter with an angle I haven't identified. Something I can't explain and don't have the vocabulary for." She pauses. Lets the options hang in the air like smoke. "But I know what you're NOT."
She lets that land. And it does — like a stone dropped into still water, the ripples spreading outward through my chest.

"You're not a thirty-two-year-old designer from Brooklyn with no documented history before 2019. You don't have a birth certificate that checks out. Your social security number traces to a woman who died in 1997 in Albuquerque. Your educational records from Central Saint Martins reference a student who attended for one semester and withdrew — a student whose photographs from that period look nothing like you."
Each sentence is a nail. Each nail is precise.
"And I know," she says, and now her voice drops half a register, now the silk parts and the steel shows, "that Rowan Ashworth is bankrolling you without knowing the truth."

The barb finds its mark. Not in my pride — that healed over centuries ago, scarred into something impervious. In my chest. In the place where Rowan's name lives, warm and close, a small fire I've been tending in the dark.
I don't react. I don't flinch. But something in my ribcage tightens, and Maren sees it. Of course she sees it. She's been watching for it.
"What do you want?"

The question leaves my mouth quieter than I intend. Not weak — I won't allow weak — but stripped of pretense. The mask of casual indifference has served its purpose; Maren has already seen past it. There's no value in maintaining the illusion for someone who's already cataloged the truth.
Maren sits back. She folds her hands in her lap. The posture of someone settling in for a negotiation, someone who has the superior position and knows it.
"I want you to withdraw from the showcase."

The words land like a physical blow. The Foundation's upcoming design showcase — my debut as a funded designer, the culmination of months of work, the moment when my collection enters the world with Rowan's name and resources behind it. The biggest moment of my career. The biggest moment of this life.

"Withdraw," Maren repeats, because she wants me to hear it twice, to feel the weight of it. "Quietly. Cite creative differences. Burnout. Artistic integrity. Choose whatever language you like — the industry loves a principled exit." She smiles, and the smile is almost gentle, which makes it worse. "And I'll keep what I know to myself."

I stare at her. The vetiver perfume curls between us, earthy and deliberate, and I think: she rehearsed this. She chose the perfume, the timing, the door-closing. She chose to come at midnight when the building was empty and there would be no witnesses and I would be tired and my defenses would be lowered. She chose to bring photographs instead of arguments because photographs don't lie and she knows that I know that.
"And if I don't?"

Maren stands. The chair scrapes softly against the concrete. She straightens the hem of her jacket — charcoal, immaculate, probably worth more than my rent — and looks down at me with an expression I can only describe as professional regret. As if she's sorry it's come to this, but not sorry enough to stop.

"Then I find out what you are." She says it simply. Not as a threat. As a statement of fact, the way one might say the sun will rise or the tide will turn. "And I share it with the world. And the world—" She pauses. Lets the silence do its work. "The fashion world, which is cruel and unforgiving and built on image, will not be kind to someone who can't explain her own face."
She picks up her bag from where she'd set it beside the chair. Slings it over her shoulder. Moves toward the door.
"Maren."
She stops. Doesn't turn.
"What makes you think I can't explain it?"

A beat. Then she looks over her shoulder, and her expression is something I haven't seen before — not calculation, not triumph, but a flicker of genuine curiosity. As if, for one moment, the predator wonders whether the prey might after all be something else entirely.
"You've had three hundred years to explain it," she says. "And you haven't."
She leaves.

The door doesn't click shut behind her. She leaves it ajar — a deliberate choice, I'm sure, a signal that she's not trapping me in, she's giving me options. The illusion of freedom. The most generous cruelty.
The phone is still on the table.

She left it there. Intentionally. The photographs are still on the screen, side by side, the 1943 café and the Met Gala steps, and the glow of the display throws pale light onto the ceiling like a small, accusatory moon.

I stare at them. My own face from eighty-three years ago. My own face from three weeks ago. The evidence is circumstantial — no DNA, no confession, no document that proves the woman in the café and the woman at the Met are the same person. A skeptic could dismantle it. A true believer could build a cathedral on it.
But Maren is neither. Maren is something more dangerous: she is convinced. And the convinced don't need proof; they need an audience.

I pick up the phone. The screen is cold under my fingers, and the cold travels up through my hand and into my wrist and settles somewhere behind my sternum. I zoom in on the 1943 photograph. The hat. The café. The way the light catches the ridge of my brow. I remember the Canadian photographer's name was Thomas. I remember that he bought me a drink afterward and asked where I'd gotten such remarkable cheekbones and I told him my grandmother was Basque and he believed me because people believe what they can file away.
I zoom in on the Met Gala photograph. The dress Rowan designed with me. The moment everything was becoming possible — the showcase, the collection, a life built on something other than disappearance.
The images burn into my retinas. I close my eyes and they're still there, afterimages in negative, the same face repeated across time like an echo that doesn't fade.

I think: I've survived the Inquisition. I have knelt on stone floors while men in vestments read my sins aloud and the air tasted of incense and terror. I've survived witch trials — the cold water, the warten, the questions that had no right answers, only slower and faster paths to the rope. I've survived centuries of people who wanted to destroy what they couldn't understand, who looked at a woman who wouldn't age and saw a monster instead of a miracle, who reached for fire because fire was the only language they had.
Maren Voss has photographs.
My tormentors had fire.
I set the phone down. My hands are steady. My heart is not.

The ultimatum is clean in its simplicity: withdraw from the showcase or be exposed. Step back into the shadows or be dragged into the light. Choose the career I've built across a dozen lifetimes or choose this one — this life, this love, this chance to be Orlando the designer instead of Orlando the fugitive.

And the thing is — the thing that tastes like metal and feels like falling — is that Maren is right about one thing. The fashion world will not be kind. This industry that trades on novelty and image and the currency of the new will not forgive the oldest thing it has ever encountered. A woman who can't explain her own face. A designer whose greatest creation is the illusion of her own existence.
I have never withdrawn from anything in four hundred years.
But I've also never had so much to lose.
